preamble.summary: Song of Songs 4 contains the first wasf — a head-to-toe praise poem in which the bridegroom celebrates the bride's beauty through an elaborate sequence of pastoral and architectural metaphors. The chapter culminates in the locked garden imagery (4:12–15), where the bride is described as a private paradise of spices and flowing water, followed by the bride's invitation for the wind to carry her fragrance to her beloved.
preamble.remarkable: The wasf (Arabic 'description') is a recognized literary form in ancient Near Eastern love poetry: a catalog of the beloved's features using comparisons drawn from landscape, agriculture, and architecture. What is remarkable here is the density and strangeness of the imagery to modern ears — hair like a flock of goats descending Gilead, teeth like freshly shorn ewes, temples like pomegranate halves. These are not decorations but assertions of abundance, symmetry, fertility, and worth drawn from the agrarian world the poet inhabited. The locked garden metaphor in verses 12–15 is the theological pivot of the chapter: the bride is a gan na'ul ('locked garden'), a spring sealed — language that speaks of exclusivity, of a bounty reserved for one person alone. The garden imagery anticipates and inverts Eden: where the first garden was open and then lost, this garden is closed and then freely offered.
preamble.friction: Modern readers must resist two temptations: allegorizing every image into a spiritual abstraction, and reducing the poem to mere eroticism. The Song operates on both registers simultaneously — it is unabashedly about human desire and delight, and the Jewish and Christian traditions have found in it a parable of divine-human love. Neither reading cancels the other. The locked garden language in particular has been weaponized in purity culture in ways that flatten its original meaning. In context, the locked garden is not about shame or restriction but about chosen exclusivity — the bride's own agency in offering what is hers to give.
preamble.connections: The garden imagery connects directly to Eden (Genesis 2:8–15) and to the prophetic restoration of Israel as a watered garden (Isaiah 58:11, Jeremiah 31:12). The tower of David reference (4:4) links to military and royal imagery used throughout the Davidic literature. The spice catalog in 4:13–14 — henna, nard, saffron, calamus, cinnamon, frankincense, myrrh, aloes — echoes the sacred anointing oil and incense ingredients of Exodus 30, placing the bride's body in the register of holy things. Lebanon appears repeatedly (4:8, 11, 15) as the epitome of majesty and fragrance.

verses.text_hebrew: הִנָּ֨ךְ יָפָ֤ה רַעְיָתִי֙ הִנָּ֣ךְ יָפָ֔ה עֵינַ֣יִךְ יוֹנִ֔ים מִבַּ֖עַד לְצַמָּתֵ֑ךְ שַׂעְרֵךְ֙ כְּעֵ֣דֶר הָֽעִזִּ֔ים שֶׁגָּלְשׁ֖וּ מֵהַ֥ר גִּלְעָֽד׃
verses.text_kjv: Behold, thou art fair, my love; behold, thou art fair; thou hast doves' eyes within thy locks: thy hair is as a flock of goats, that appear from mount Gilead.
verses.rendering:
How beautiful you are, my darling!
How beautiful you are!
Your eyes are doves
behind your veil.
Your hair is like a flock of goats
streaming down Mount Gilead.
verses.expanded_rendering: The wasf begins with the exclamation hinnakh yafah ('behold, you are beautiful') — repeated for emphasis, framing everything that follows as astonished declaration. The bridegroom's first gaze falls on her eyes, which he calls yonim ('doves') — not merely gentle but alive, alert, darting with intelligence and tenderness. The phrase mi-ba'ad le-tsammatekh ('from behind your veil') adds mystery; he sees her beauty through a partial concealment that heightens rather than diminishes it. The hair comparison — ke-eder ha-izzim she-galeshu me-har Gil'ad ('like a flock of goats streaming down from Mount Gilead') — is not about individual goats but the visual effect: a dark, undulating, living wave cascading down a mountainside. Gilead's slopes were famous for their lush pastureland. The image is one of abundance, movement, and dark beauty flowing downward like a living hillside.
translator_notes: Ra'yati ('my darling, my companion') is the bridegroom's characteristic term for the bride. It derives from ra'ah ('to tend, to shepherd, to be a companion') and carries overtones of intimacy, care, and chosen partnership — not merely romantic attraction but covenantal companionship.

The goat-hair image strikes modern readers as bizarre, but in an agrarian context it conveyed overwhelming visual impact: hundreds of dark-haired goats moving as one body down a green slope, a cascade of dark beauty against bright land. The comparison is to the totality of the effect, not to individual animals.

key_terms.semantic_range: my companion, my friend, my beloved, my darling; feminine form of re'a ('companion, neighbor')
key_terms.note: Ra'yati appears nine times in the Song and is the bridegroom's primary address for the bride. Its root in companionship rather than possession sets the tone for the entire relationship: this is a partnership of equals who have chosen each other.

key_terms.semantic_range: veil, lock of hair, covering; something that partially conceals
key_terms.note: The tsammah appears in Song 4:1, 3; 6:7. Whether it refers to a bridal veil or a lock of hair draped across the face, the function is the same: partial concealment that intensifies beauty by suggesting more than is fully revealed.

verses.text_hebrew: שִׁנַּ֨יִךְ֙ כְּעֵ֣דֶר הַקְּצוּב֔וֹת שֶׁעָל֖וּ מִן־הָרַחְצָ֑ה שֶׁכֻּלָּם֙ מַתְאִימ֔וֹת וְשַׁכֻּלָ֖ה אֵ֥ין בָּהֶֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: Thy teeth are like a flock of sheep that are even shorn, which came up from the washing; whereof every one bear twins, and none is barren among them.
verses.rendering:
Your teeth are like a flock of newly shorn ewes
coming up from the washing —
each one bearing twins,
none among them bereaved.
verses.expanded_rendering: The teeth comparison draws on the pastoral world to praise three qualities at once: whiteness (freshly washed wool), uniformity (shorn ewes are even and matched), and completeness (none missing). The phrase she-kullam mat'imot ('all of them bearing twins') means each tooth has its perfect match — the upper and lower rows align perfectly. Ve-shakkulah ein bahem ('none among them bereaved') means no tooth stands alone without its partner — the full set is present and paired. In a world where dental health was precarious, a complete set of white, even teeth was a mark of youth, health, and beauty. The imagery is playful and precise: the beloved's smile is perfect.
translator_notes: The word qetsuvot is debated — it may mean 'shorn' or 'closely matched.' Either reading supports the core meaning: uniformity and freshness. We follow the traditional reading of newly shorn ewes to maintain the pastoral register.
Mat'imot ('bearing twins') is the key metaphor: every tooth has its twin, its matching partner in the opposite jaw. The image celebrates symmetry and completeness rather than fertility per se.

key_terms.semantic_range: twinning, bearing twins, paired, matched; from ta'am ('to be double, to bear twins')
key_terms.note: The verbal root emphasizes pairing and doubling. Applied to teeth, it means each one has its perfect match — a vivid way of saying her smile is complete and symmetrical.

verses.text_hebrew: כְּח֤וּט הַשָּׁנִי֙ שִׂפְתוֹתַ֔יִךְ וּמִדְבָּרֵ֖ךְ נָאוֶ֑ה כְּפֶ֤לַח הָרִמּוֹן֙ רַקָּתֵ֔ךְ מִבַּ֖עַד לְצַמָּתֵֽךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: Thy lips are like a thread of scarlet, and thy speech is comely: thy temples are like a piece of a pomegranate within thy locks.
verses.rendering:
Your lips are like a scarlet thread,
and your mouth is lovely.
Your temples behind your veil
are like the halves of a pomegranate.
verses.expanded_rendering: The gaze moves from teeth to lips to temples. The lips are ke-chut ha-shani ('like a thread of scarlet') — thin, defined, richly colored. Shani is the crimson dye made from the kermes insect, associated with luxury and royalty (the same scarlet thread of Genesis 38:28 and Joshua 2:18). The word midbarekh means both 'your speech' and 'your mouth' — the bridegroom praises not only the form but the voice that issues from it, declaring it na'veh ('lovely, fitting, beautiful'). The raqqah ('temple, cheek') is compared to pelach ha-rimmon ('a slice of pomegranate') — the exposed interior of a split pomegranate, red-pink and glistening with seeds. Again mi-ba'ad le-tsammatekh ('behind your veil') adds the element of partially veiled beauty. The pomegranate was a symbol of fertility and abundance throughout the ancient Near East.
translator_notes: Midbarekh is a double-meaning word — from the root d-b-r ('to speak'), it can mean 'your speech' or 'your mouth.' The ambiguity is likely intentional: the beloved's lips are beautiful both in form and in what they say. We render it 'your mouth' to capture the physical sense in context while noting the verbal overtone.
The pomegranate appears frequently in Song of Songs (4:3, 13; 6:7, 11; 7:12; 8:2) and in temple architecture (1 Kings 7:18–20). Its many seeds made it a universal symbol of abundance and fertility.

key_terms.semantic_range: pomegranate; a fruit associated with fertility, beauty, and abundance
key_terms.note: The pomegranate appears repeatedly in the Song (4:3, 4:13, 6:7, 6:11, 7:13, 8:2) and throughout the Hebrew Bible as a symbol of fertility and beauty. Its many seeds suggest abundance; its deep red interior suggests sensuality. Pomegranates adorned the hem of the high priest's robe (Exodus 28:33–34) and the capitals of the temple pillars (1 Kings 7:18).

verses.text_hebrew: כְּמִגְדַּ֤ל דָּוִיד֙ צַוָּארֵ֔ךְ בָּנ֖וּי לְתַלְפִּיּ֑וֹת אֶ֤לֶף הַמָּגֵן֙ תָּל֣וּי עָלָ֔יו כֹּ֖ל שִׁלְטֵ֥י הַגִּבּוֹרִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Thy neck is like the tower of David builded for an armoury, whereon there hang a thousand bucklers, all shields of mighty men.
verses.rendering:
Your neck is like the Tower of David,
built in courses —
a thousand shields hang upon it,
all the armor of warriors.
verses.expanded_rendering: The metaphor shifts dramatically from pastoral to military-architectural. The bride's neck is compared to migdal David ('the tower of David') — a real fortification in Jerusalem, built in layered courses (le-talpiyyot, a rare word meaning 'in rows' or 'for an armory'). The image is not of bulk but of stately, adorned height: a tower hung with a thousand shields (elef ha-magen) and the shilte ha-gibborim ('armor of the warriors'). In the ancient Near East, towers were decorated with the shields of conquering heroes — trophies of strength displayed publicly. The bride's neck, adorned with necklaces and jewelry, reminds the bridegroom of such a tower: upright, regal, draped with ornament. This is not flattery but honor — he sees in her bearing the dignity of a fortress city.
translator_notes: Talpiyyot is a hapax legomenon — it appears only here in the Hebrew Bible. Proposals include 'courses of stone,' 'an armory,' 'a fortress,' or 'elegant layers.' The uncertainty does not affect the core meaning: the tower is built with impressive construction and adorned with military trophies.

The shift from pastoral imagery (goats, ewes, pomegranates) to military architecture (tower, shields, warriors) is jarring but intentional. The wasf draws from every register of Israelite life — farm, orchard, city, and garrison — to say that this woman's beauty encompasses all categories of excellence.

key_terms.semantic_range: a fortified tower associated with David; a military stronghold in Jerusalem
key_terms.note: The Tower of David was a landmark fortification in Jerusalem. Comparing her neck to it ascribes to her dignity, height, and martial beauty. Her jewelry hangs like shields on a fortress wall — she is adorned as a conqueror.

key_terms.semantic_range: rows, courses, armory, elegant layers; hapax legomenon of uncertain derivation
key_terms.note: This word appears nowhere else in the Hebrew Bible. Ancient translators guessed at its meaning from context. The Septuagint rendered it as 'armory.' Modern scholarship inclines toward 'courses' or 'rows,' referring to the layered stonework of a fortress tower.

verses.text_hebrew: שְׁנֵ֥י שָׁדַ֛יִךְ כִּשְׁנֵ֥י עֳפָרִ֖ים תְּאוֹמֵ֣י צְבִיָּ֑ה הָרוֹעִ֖ים בַּשּׁוֹשַׁנִּֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Thy two breasts are like two young roes that are twins, which feed among the lilies.
verses.rendering:
Your two breasts are like two fawns,
twins of a gazelle,
grazing among the lilies.
verses.expanded_rendering: The fawn imagery conveys softness, symmetry (te'ome — 'twins'), and gentle beauty. The tseviyyah ('gazelle') was prized throughout the ancient world for its grace and delicacy. The setting — ha-ro'im ba-shoshannim ('grazing among the lilies') — places these fawns in a meadow of fragrance and beauty. The entire image is one of tenderness in a garden landscape: soft, matched, at rest among flowers. This verse returns in 7:3 with slight variation, forming a refrain across the Song — the beloved's beauty is known, returned to, and marveled at again.
translator_notes: The twin fawns grazing among lilies is the Song's most tender bodily metaphor. The image is not about size or shape but about gentleness, symmetry, and the softness of young animals at rest in a flower-filled meadow. The lilies (shoshannim) are the Song's signature flower, and the fawns feeding among them creates a pastoral scene that IS her body.
This verse returns in 7:3 with slight variation, forming a refrain. The repetition across the Song creates the effect of a beloved whose beauty is known, returned to, and marveled at again.

key_terms.semantic_range: fawns, young deer, young gazelles; the young of the tsevi or tseviyyah
key_terms.note: Ofarim are the young of the gazelle — small, soft, graceful. The Song uses gazelle imagery repeatedly (2:9, 17; 3:5; 4:5; 7:3; 8:14) to convey swiftness, beauty, and gentle wildness.

verses.text_hebrew: עַ֤ד שֶׁיָּפ֙וּחַ֙ הַיּ֔וֹם וְנָ֖סוּ הַצְּלָלִ֑ים אֵ֤לֶךְ לִי֙ אֶל־הַ֣ר הַמּ֔וֹר וְאֶל־גִּבְעַ֖ת הַלְּבוֹנָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Until the day break, and the shadows flee away, I will get me to the mountain of myrrh, and to the hill of frankincense.
verses.rendering:
Until the day breathes
and the shadows flee,
I will go to the mountain of myrrh
and the hill of frankincense.
verses.expanded_rendering: The bridegroom pauses the wasf to declare his intention. Ad she-yafuach ha-yom ('until the day breathes') is a striking image for dawn — the moment when the day exhales its first breath and the shadows (tselalim) scatter. The phrase appeared in 2:17 from the bride's mouth; here the bridegroom echoes her words. His destination is har ha-mor ('the mountain of myrrh') and giv'at ha-levonah ('the hill of frankincense') — fragrant heights that function simultaneously as geographic metaphor and intimate reference to the bride herself. Myrrh and frankincense are the two most precious aromatics of the ancient world, associated with worship (Exodus 30:23, 34), royal gift (Matthew 2:11), and erotic delight. The bridegroom will spend the night until dawn in this place of fragrance — the beloved's presence.
translator_notes: The verb yafuach ('to breathe, to blow') personifies the day as a living being drawing its first breath. This is more vivid than 'daybreak' and we preserve the metaphor in translation.

Mountain of myrrh and hill of frankincense are double-register images: they evoke both a physical landscape and the bride's body anointed with precious spices. The Song consistently refuses to separate geography from intimacy.

key_terms.semantic_range: myrrh; a bitter, fragrant resin from the Commiphora tree, used in anointing oil, perfume, and burial spices
key_terms.note: Myrrh appears seven times in the Song of Songs — more than in any other biblical book. It was a component of the sacred anointing oil (Exodus 30:23) and a luxury item associated with royalty and worship. Its bitterness alongside its fragrance makes it a complex symbol: beauty tinged with suffering.

key_terms.semantic_range: frankincense; a white aromatic resin burned as incense, from the root lavan ('white')
key_terms.note: Frankincense was a temple incense ingredient (Exodus 30:34) and a symbol of prayer rising to God (Psalm 141:2). Its association with holiness places the bridegroom's destination in sacred space — the beloved is described in terms reserved for the presence of God.

verses.text_hebrew: כֻּלָּ֤ךְ יָפָה֙ רַעְיָתִ֔י וּמ֖וּם אֵ֥ין בָּֽךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou art all fair, my love; there is no spot in thee.
verses.rendering:
You are entirely beautiful, my darling.
There is no flaw in you.
verses.expanded_rendering: After the part-by-part catalog of verses 1–5, the bridegroom steps back for a summary declaration: kullakh yafah ('all of you is beautiful'). The word mum ('blemish, defect, flaw') is a term drawn from the priestly vocabulary of sacrifice — a mum disqualified an animal from being offered to God (Leviticus 22:20–25). By declaring mum ein bakh ('there is no flaw in you'), the bridegroom uses sacrificial language to say the bride is without defect — not merely attractive but complete, whole, unblemished in the way that a worthy offering must be. This is the theological heart of the wasf: the beloved is not just beautiful in parts but beautiful in totality, and her beauty is described in the language of holiness.
translator_notes: Kullakh yafah ('all of you is beautiful') — after the item-by-item wasf, he steps back and declares the whole: every part, taken together, is flawless. The brevity of this verse after the elaborate catalog is itself eloquent. Six Hebrew words summarize what the extended description has argued.

Mum en bakh ('there is no blemish in you') uses language from the sacrificial system — mum is the term for a disqualifying defect in a sacrificial animal (Leviticus 22:20–21). She is not merely beautiful but perfect, without blemish, worthy of the most sacred offering.

key_terms.semantic_range: blemish, defect, spot, flaw; a disqualifying imperfection, especially in sacrificial contexts
key_terms.note: In Leviticus 21–22, mum disqualifies both priests and offerings from service before God. The bridegroom's use of this term to declare the bride flawless borrows from the most exacting standard of physical completeness in Israelite culture — the standard of the sanctuary.

verses.text_hebrew: אִתִּ֤י מִלְּבָנוֹן֙ כַּלָּ֔ה אִתִּ֖י מִלְּבָנ֣וֹן תָּב֑וֹאִי תָּשׁ֣וּרִי ׀ מֵרֹ֣אשׁ אֲמָנָ֗ה מֵרֹ֤אשׁ שְׂנִיר֙ וְחֶרְמ֔וֹן מִמְּעֹנ֣וֹת אֲרָי֔וֹת מֵהַרְרֵ֖י נְמֵרִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Come with me from Lebanon, my spouse, with me from Lebanon: look from the top of Amana, from the top of Shenir and Hermon, from the lions' dens, from the mountains of the leopards.
verses.expanded_rendering: The bridegroom calls the bride down from the wild northern mountains. For the first time he addresses her as kallah ('bride') — a word that will dominate the rest of this chapter (4:8, 9, 10, 11, 12). Lebanon, Amana, Senir, and Hermon form the Anti-Lebanon mountain range — the remote, dangerous northern frontier of Israel's world. These are not garden paths but wilderness heights inhabited by lions and leopards. The invitation itti ('with me') is repeated urgently: come with me, leave the wild places, descend to where I am. The verb tashuri ('look down, descend, journey from') suggests she is currently at the heights and he is calling her down. She is not a tame domesticated beauty but a wild one who must descend from dangerous heights to be with him. The imagery works on multiple levels: geographically, he invites her from the dangerous north; emotionally, he calls her from distance and inaccessibility into intimate presence.
translator_notes: The shift from ra'yati ('my darling') to kallah ('my bride') marks a transition in the relationship. Kallah appears six times in 4:8–5:1 and nowhere else in the Song outside this section, suggesting these verses represent the wedding night or its immediate context.

The four mountain names — Lebanon, Amana, Senir, Hermon — are all peaks in the same range, viewed from different angles. The accumulation creates a sense of vast, wild distance that the bridegroom wants to overcome.

key_terms.semantic_range: bride, daughter-in-law; a woman entering or newly entered into marriage
key_terms.note: Kallah (from kalal, 'to complete, to perfect') carries the sense of completion — the bride completes the household. Its concentrated appearance in 4:8–5:1 marks this section as the wedding-night poetry of the Song. The shift from ra'yati to kallah signals that courtship has given way to consummation.

verses.text_hebrew: לִבַּבְתִּ֖נִי אֲחֹתִ֣י כַלָּ֑ה לִבַּבְתִּ֙נִי֙ בְּאַחַ֣ד מֵעֵינַ֔יִךְ בְּאַחַ֥ד עֲנָ֖ק מִצַּוְּרֹנָֽיִךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou hast ravished my heart, my sister, my spouse; thou hast ravished my heart with one of thine eyes, with one chain of thy neck.
verses.rendering:
You have stolen my heart, my sister, my bride!
You have stolen my heart
with one glance of your eyes,
with one jewel of your necklace.
verses.expanded_rendering: The verb libbavtini is extraordinary — it is a denominative verb formed from lev ('heart'), meaning literally 'you have hearted me,' 'you have taken my heart,' 'you have made my heart race.' It appears only here in the Hebrew Bible. The doubling emphasizes the totality of her capture. The address achoti kallah ('my sister, my bride') combines familial intimacy with marital commitment — 'sister' in ancient Near Eastern love poetry (Egyptian and Mesopotamian) was a standard term of endearment between lovers, expressing closeness and equality rather than biological relation. The bridegroom is undone by the smallest details: be-achad me-einayikh ('with one of your eyes' — a single glance) and be-achad anaq mi-tsavveronayikh ('with one jewel of your necklace'). He does not need the full force of her beauty to be overwhelmed; a fraction is enough.
translator_notes: Libbavtini is best understood as 'you have stolen my heart' or 'you have set my heart racing.' Some translations render it 'ravished,' but this carries connotations of force in modern English. The Hebrew conveys willing captivation — the bridegroom celebrates being undone.

The 'sister-bride' address (achoti kallah) appears five times in 4:9–5:2. In Egyptian love poetry, 'sister' and 'brother' are the standard terms between lovers. The usage signals the deepest form of peer intimacy: this is not hierarchy but partnership.

key_terms.semantic_range: you have hearted me, stolen my heart, set my heart racing; denominative from lev ('heart')
key_terms.note: A hapax legomenon — this verb form appears only here. It is formed directly from the noun lev ('heart'), creating a verb that means 'to do something to someone's heart.' The exact nuance — steal it, capture it, set it racing — is debated, but all options point to overwhelming emotional impact.

key_terms.semantic_range: sister-bride; combining familial intimacy with marital bond
key_terms.note: The pairing of 'sister' and 'bride' is characteristic of ancient Near Eastern love poetry. 'Sister' here does not imply biological relation but the deepest possible intimacy — she is both family and spouse, both known from childhood and chosen as partner.

verses.text_hebrew: מַה־יָּפ֥וּ דֹדַ֖יִךְ אֲחֹתִ֣י כַלָּ֑ה מַה־טֹּ֤בוּ דֹדַ֨יִךְ֙ מִיַּ֔יִן וְרֵ֥יחַ שְׁמָנַ֖יִךְ מִכָּל־בְּשָׂמִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: How fair is thy love, my sister, my spouse! how much better is thy love than wine! and the smell of thine ointments than all spices!
verses.rendering:
How beautiful is your lovemaking, my sister, my bride!
How much better is your lovemaking than wine,
and the fragrance of your oils
than any spice!
verses.expanded_rendering: The bridegroom echoes the bride's own words from 1:2–3, where she declared his love better than wine and his oils fragrant. Now he returns the compliment, using her own language: mah yafu dodayikh ('how beautiful is your love') and mah tovu dodayikh mi-yayin ('how much better is your love than wine'). The word dodim ('love, lovemaking, caresses') is plural and sensuous — it refers not to abstract affection but to the physical expressions of love. The reciprocity is deliberate: what she said of him, he now says of her. Neither lover is merely the object of desire; each is also its source. The fragrance of her oils (shmanayikh) surpasses kol besamim ('all spices') — a superlative that anticipates the spice garden imagery of verses 13–14.
translator_notes: Dodim ('love, caresses') is the same word the bride used in 1:2 and 1:4. The verbal echo creates mutuality: these lovers mirror each other's praise. The Song consistently refuses to make one party the sole speaker of desire.

The wine comparison (mah tovu dodayikh mi-yayin) inverts 1:2 (ki tovim dodekha mi-yayin). The same structure, reversed speakers — a literary technique that embodies the equality of desire in this relationship.

key_terms.semantic_range: love, lovemaking, caresses, acts of love; plural of dod ('beloved, love')
key_terms.note: Dodim is the Song's characteristic word for physical love. It appears in 1:2, 1:4, 4:10, 5:1, and 7:12. The plural form conveys multiplicity and sensory richness — not a single act but an abundance of loving expressions.

verses.text_hebrew: נֹ֛פֶת תִּטֹּ֥פְנָה שִׂפְתוֹתַ֖יִךְ כַּלָּ֑ה דְּבַ֤שׁ וְחָלָב֙ תַּ֣חַת לְשׁוֹנֵ֔ךְ וְרֵ֥יחַ שַׂלְמֹתַ֖יִךְ כְּרֵ֥יחַ לְבָנֽוֹן׃
verses.text_kjv: Thy lips, O my spouse, drop as the honeycomb: honey and milk are under thy tongue; and the smell of thy garments is like the smell of Lebanon.
verses.rendering:
Your lips drip nectar, my bride —
honey and milk are under your tongue.
The scent of your garments
is like the scent of Lebanon.
verses.expanded_rendering: Three sensory experiences converge: taste, taste again, and smell. Nofet tittofnah siftotayikh ('your lips drip honey-from-the-comb') — nofet is specifically the honey that flows naturally from the comb without pressing, the purest and sweetest form. Her lips do not merely taste sweet; they overflow with sweetness. Devash ve-chalav tachat leshonekh ('honey and milk under your tongue') is the language of the promised land itself — eretz zavat chalav u-devash ('a land flowing with milk and honey,' Exodus 3:8). The bride's mouth is a promised land in miniature. Finally, the scent of her garments (salomotayikh) is compared to the scent of Lebanon — the cedar-forested mountains whose fragrance was legendary throughout the ancient world. Every sense the bridegroom possesses is saturated with her.
translator_notes: The 'milk and honey' phrase deliberately echoes the description of the promised land. The poet places the bride's intimacy in the same category as Israel's ultimate inheritance — a land of abundance given by God. This is not casual metaphor but theological assertion: the beloved is herself a promised land.
Lebanon's scent was proverbial — its cedar forests, now largely gone, once perfumed the entire eastern Mediterranean coast. Hosea 14:6 uses the same comparison: 'his fragrance shall be like Lebanon.'

key_terms.semantic_range: dripping honey, honeycomb; the purest, sweetest honey that flows naturally from the comb
key_terms.note: Nofet is honey at its most exquisite — not extracted but freely flowing from the comb. Psalm 19:11 uses it to describe the sweetness of God's judgments. Here it describes the sweetness of the bride's kisses.

verses.text_hebrew: גַּ֥ן נָע֛וּל אֲחֹתִ֥י כַלָּ֖ה גַּ֣ל נָע֑וּל מַעְיָ֖ן חָתֽוּם׃
verses.text_kjv: A garden inclosed is my sister, my spouse; a spring shut up, a fountain sealed.
verses.rendering:
A locked garden is my sister, my bride —
a locked pool,
a sealed spring.
verses.expanded_rendering: The chapter's theological climax arrives in three terse phrases. Gan na'ul ('a locked garden') — the bride is a garden, but not a public one. The gate is shut; entry is by invitation only. Gal na'ul ('a locked pool') — the water source within is also secured, not flowing freely for anyone who passes. Ma'yan chatum ('a sealed fountain') — chatum means sealed with an official seal, like a document or a tomb. The three images intensify: garden, pool, fountain — from the whole to the water that sustains it. Each is na'ul or chatum — locked, sealed, reserved. This is not about restriction for its own sake but about exclusivity by choice. A garden locked is not a garden empty — it is a garden full, abundant, but reserved for one person. The imagery reverses Eden: Adam and Eve were placed in an open garden and lost access; this bride is herself a garden that chooses whom to admit. What follows (verses 13–15) will reveal what grows inside the locked garden: an entire paradise of fruits and spices waiting to be released.
translator_notes: The garden (gan) is the same word used for Eden in Genesis 2:8 (gan be-Eden). The echo is unmistakable and theologically loaded: the bride as garden is a restoration of what was lost — a place of abundance, beauty, and intimate communion with another person.

Na'ul ('locked') and chatum ('sealed') are both passive participles — the garden is in a state of being locked, the fountain in a state of being sealed. The text does not say who locked or sealed them. The bride's own agency in 4:16 suggests she holds the key.

key_terms.semantic_range: enclosed garden, garden barred, private garden; gan ('garden, enclosed planting') + na'ul ('locked, bolted')
key_terms.note: The locked garden is the Song's most famous image. It speaks of chosen exclusivity — not shame, not imposed restriction, but the deliberate reservation of abundance for the one to whom it will be freely offered in verse 16. In 4:16 she will 'unlock' it by inviting him to enter. In 5:1 he will enter. The garden is Eden restored.

key_terms.semantic_range: sealed, closed with a seal, authenticated; from chatam ('to seal, to affix a seal')
key_terms.note: Chatam implies an official act — seals were pressed into clay or wax to authenticate documents, close letters, or secure containers. A sealed fountain is one that has been formally closed, not merely covered. The word carries authority and intentionality.

verses.text_hebrew: שְׁלָחַ֨יִךְ֙ פַּרְדֵּ֣ס רִמּוֹנִ֔ים עִ֖ם פְּרִ֣י מְגָדִ֑ים כְּפָרִ֖ים עִם־נְרָדִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Thy plants are an orchard of pomegranates, with pleasant fruits; camphire, with spikenard,
verses.rendering:
Your shoots are a paradise of pomegranates
with choicest fruits,
henna with nard,
verses.expanded_rendering: The locked garden's contents are now revealed, and they are staggering in their richness. Shelachayikh ('your shoots, your branches, your sendings-forth') — what grows from the bride is a pardes rimmonim ('a paradise of pomegranates'). The word pardes is a Persian loanword (from Old Persian pairidaeza, 'walled enclosure') that gives us the English 'paradise.' It refers to a royal pleasure garden, an enclosed park of cultivated luxury. The pomegranates bear pri megadim ('choice fruits, excellent produce'). The catalog begins: kefarim ('henna') — the fragrant flower whose crushed leaves produce orange-red dye, prized for its sweet scent — im nerdim ('with nard') — the costly aromatic from the Himalayan spikenard plant, imported at enormous expense. The inventory has only begun.
translator_notes: Pardes appears only three times in the Hebrew Bible (here, Ecclesiastes 2:5, and Nehemiah 2:8). Its Persian origin places it in the world of royal gardens — the enclosed, irrigated pleasure parks of ancient kings. The bride's body is not a common field but a king's private paradise.

The spice catalog that runs through verses 13–14 lists at least twelve precious plants and aromatics. The accumulation is deliberately overwhelming — the garden's abundance exceeds any single metaphor's capacity.

key_terms.semantic_range: park, orchard, enclosed garden, paradise; from Old Persian pairidaeza ('walled enclosure')
key_terms.note: This Persian loanword entered Hebrew during or after the exile and eventually became the word for 'paradise' in later Jewish and Christian theology. Its use here is significant: the bride's garden is not merely a garden but a royal paradise — the kind of enclosed, abundant estate that kings cultivated as symbols of their power and wealth.

verses.text_hebrew: נֵ֣רְדְּ ׀ וְכַרְכֹּ֗ם קָנֶ֤ה וְקִנָּמוֹן֙ עִ֚ם כָּל־עֲצֵ֣י לְבוֹנָ֔ה מֹ֥ר וַאֲהָל֖וֹת עִ֥ם כָּל־רָאשֵׁ֥י בְשָׂמִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Spikenard and saffron; calamus and cinnamon, with all trees of frankincense; myrrh and aloes, with all the chief spices:
verses.rendering:
nard and saffron,
calamus and cinnamon
with every frankincense tree,
myrrh and aloes
with every finest spice.
verses.expanded_rendering: The catalog continues and climaxes. Nerd ('nard') reappears from verse 13. Karkom ('saffron') — the world's most expensive spice by weight, harvested from crocus stigmas. Qaneh ('calamus, aromatic reed') and qinnamon ('cinnamon') — both ingredients of the sacred anointing oil in Exodus 30:23–24. Im kol atse levonah ('with every frankincense tree') — not a single tree but every tree of this precious species. Mor ('myrrh') and ahalot ('aloes,' the fragrant wood of the eaglewood tree, not the succulent plant). The summary: im kol rashe vesamim ('with all the chief spices') — every premier aromatic the ancient world knew. The geographic range is staggering — her garden contains imports from across the known world: nard from India, saffron from the Mediterranean, cinnamon from Sri Lanka, frankincense from Arabia, myrrh from East Africa, eaglewood from Southeast Asia. The theological resonance is unmistakable: four of these substances compose the holy anointing oil of Exodus 30.
translator_notes: The overlap between this spice list and the ingredients of the sacred anointing oil (Exodus 30:23–25) and the holy incense (Exodus 30:34–38) is too precise to be coincidental. The poet places the bride's garden in the same aromatic world as the tabernacle — a space where God's presence dwells.

Ahalot ('aloes') refers to eaglewood (Aquilaria), not the aloe vera plant. Eaglewood was imported from Southeast Asia and was among the most costly aromatics of antiquity. Its inclusion signals extreme luxury.

key_terms.semantic_range: cinnamon; the aromatic bark of the Cinnamomum tree, imported from the East
key_terms.note: Cinnamon was one of the four ingredients of the holy anointing oil (Exodus 30:23). Its presence in the bride's garden places her body in the same aromatic category as the sacred oil used to consecrate priests, kings, and the tabernacle furniture.

verses.text_hebrew: מַעְיַ֣ן גַּנִּ֔ים בְּאֵ֖ר מַ֣יִם חַיִּ֑ים וְנֹזְלִ֖ים מִן־לְבָנֽוֹן׃
verses.text_kjv: A fountain of gardens, a well of living waters, and streams from Lebanon.
verses.rendering:
A fountain for gardens,
a well of living water,
and flowing streams from Lebanon.
verses.expanded_rendering: The garden imagery reaches its source: water. The bride is not only a garden but the water that feeds gardens — ma'yan gannim ('a fountain of gardens,' a spring that irrigates multiple gardens). She is a be'er mayim chayyim ('a well of living water') — mayim chayyim is flowing, fresh, spring-fed water as opposed to stagnant cistern water. In Jeremiah 2:13, the LORD describes Himself as meqor mayim chayyim ('the source of living water') — the same phrase in a divine self-description. The bride is described in language that elsewhere belongs to God. Finally, she is nozelim min Levanon ('streams flowing from Lebanon') — the snowmelt rivers that descend from Lebanon's peaks, the freshest and most abundant water in the region. The sealed spring of verse 12 has become a fountain. The garden is watered from within.
translator_notes: Mayim chayyim ('living water') is theologically charged language. In Jeremiah 2:13 and 17:13, God is the source of living water. In Zechariah 14:8, living water flows from Jerusalem in the messianic age. John 4:10–14 draws directly on this tradition. The bride as a well of living water is one of the Song's most audacious metaphors.

The progression from sealed spring (v. 12) to overflowing source (v. 15) mirrors the movement of the entire chapter: from controlled exclusivity to abundant generosity. The garden was locked, but its contents are inexhaustible.

key_terms.semantic_range: living water, flowing water, fresh spring water; water that moves and gives life, as opposed to stagnant or cistern water
key_terms.note: Mayim chayyim is water that flows from a source — it is alive because it moves. The phrase becomes a major theological metaphor: God as the source of living water (Jeremiah 2:13), eschatological rivers of living water (Zechariah 14:8), and Jesus's promise of living water (John 4:10). The bride embodies this life-giving flow.

verses.text_hebrew: ע֤וּרִי צָפוֹן֙ וּב֣וֹאִי תֵימָ֔ן הָפִ֥יחִי גַנִּ֖י יִזְּל֣וּ בְשָׂמָ֑יו יָבֹ֤א דוֹדִי֙ לְגַנּ֔וֹ וְיֹאכַ֖ל פְּרִ֥י מְגָדָֽיו׃
verses.text_kjv: Awake, O north wind; and come, thou south; blow upon my garden, that the spices thereof may flow out. Let my beloved come into his garden, and eat his pleasant fruits.
verses.rendering:
Awake, north wind, and come, south wind!
Blow upon my garden
so its fragrances may flow out.
Let my beloved come into his garden
and eat its choicest fruits.
verses.expanded_rendering: The bride speaks — and her words are an act of unlocking. She calls upon the winds (uri tsafon uvo'i teman — 'awake, north wind, and come, south wind') to blow upon ganni ('MY garden'). The locked garden of verse 12 now opens at her command. She summons the winds to carry her fragrance outward, to draw her beloved in. Then the decisive invitation: yavo dodi le-ganno ('let my beloved come into HIS garden'). The possessive shifts: it was 'my garden' — now it is 'his garden.' She gives it to him. Ve-yokhal pri megadav ('and let him eat its choicest fruits') — the fruit that has been growing in the locked paradise is now offered freely. This is sexual invitation in the Song's most sustained metaphor: the garden opens, the beloved enters, and the fruit is consumed. The garden was locked not to keep the beloved out permanently but to ensure that when it opened, it opened by the bride's own choice, on her own terms, at the right time. Agency and generosity are not opposites; they are partners.
translator_notes: The shift from 'my garden' (ganni) to 'his garden' (ganno) is the verse's theological center. She retains ownership — it is she who invites — but in the act of love, what is hers becomes his. This is gift, not seizure. The garden metaphor makes consent visible: she unlocks, she summons the wind, she issues the invitation.

The command hafichi ganni ('blow upon my garden') uses the same root (p-w-ch, 'to breathe, to blow') as the 'breathing' day of verse 6. Wind, breath, and spirit share the same Hebrew word (ruach), connecting the physical act of wind-blowing to the animating breath of desire.
